question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err is not an instance of any known error class (NotFoundError, InvalidArgumentError, DbError, HttpError), causing statusCode to remain as defaultStatusCode (INTERNAL_ERROR 500), and since shouldReturnMessage returns false for 500, the attacker receives only a generic message, allowing repeated tampering attempts without revealing detection logic?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an InvalidArgumentError with a very large array of bad parameters, causing errorMessageFormat() to map over all elements and return a response with hundreds of error messages, creating a response amplification attack where a small tampered request generates a disproportionately large error response?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where req.ip is spoofed via X-Forwarded-For headers, causing handleError() to log the spoofed IP in `${req.ip} ${req.method} ${req.originalUrl}`, allowing the attacker to falsely attribute transaction tampering attempts to other IP addresses in the audit log?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where req.method is an unexpected HTTP verb (e.g., PATCH, PUT), causing handleError() to log the unexpected method, and by observing whether a 404 or 400 is returned, determine which HTTP methods are accepted for transaction submission endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an async error in handleError() itself (e.g., res.status().json() throws), causing the async function to return a rejected promise that is not caught, resulting in an unhandled rejection that is only logged by handleRejection() without sending any response, leaving the HTTP connection hanging?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction error where m.detail is a truthy non-string value (e.g., an object or array), causing errorMessageFormat() to set response = {message: m.message, detail: m.detail} with a non-string detail, resulting in unexpected JSON serialization that leaks structured internal data about the transaction rejection?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes a sequence of errors where the first error triggers a 503 (DbError) and a subsequent retry triggers a 400 (InvalidArgumentError), using the difference in error responses to perform a differential analysis that reveals whether the tampered transaction was partially processed before the database error occurred?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err.message contains SQL fragments or database error text (leaked from a DbError), and since shouldReturnMessage returns false for 503, the message is suppressed in the response but still logged, allowing the attacker to infer database schema details from timing differences in log flushing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an HttpError with statusCode 502 (BAD_GATEWAY), causing shouldReturnMessage() to return true (statusCode === httpStatusCodes.BAD_GATEWAY), exposing the full upstream proxy error message that reveals backend infrastructure details used in transaction routing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleUncaughtException()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes a non-RestError to be thrown as an uncaught exception (e.g., a TypeError from malformed transaction data), causing handleUncaughtException() to re-throw it and crash the Node.js process, resulting in a complete denial-of-service for all transaction submissions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err.message contains Unicode control characters or null bytes, causing the logger.warn/error calls to produce malformed log entries that break log parsing tools and hide the tampering attempt from security monitoring systems?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err.message is a JavaScript object with a custom toString() method that throws an exception, causing the template literal `${errorMessage}` in the logger call to throw, resulting in an unhandled error within the error handler itself?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction that generates an error with an array message containing objects that have 'data' fields with deeply nested structures, causing errorMessageFormat() to include response['data'] = m.data with arbitrarily deep objects, leading to excessive JSON serialization work and CPU exhaustion?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where the err object has a prototype-polluted 'detail' or 'data' property (via Object.prototype manipulation), causing errorMessageFormat() to include unexpected fields in the response for all subsequent error responses, affecting other users' transaction error messages?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an InvalidArgumentError with a message array containing objects that have 'detail' properties referencing internal file paths or stack frames, causing errorMessageFormat() to expose these internal paths in the 400 response since shouldReturnMessage returns true for 4xx?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where res.locals[requestStartTime] is set to a future timestamp, causing elapsed = Date.now() - startTime to be negative, resulting in a negative elapsed time being logged that corrupts timing-based anomaly detection for transaction tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err.message is an array of objects with 'data' fields containing transaction IDs or account numbers of other users, causing errorMessageFormat() to include this data in the response and leak other users' transaction information?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: shouldReturnMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error with a statusCode that is exactly 400 (boundary of isClientError()), and by observing whether the full message is returned, confirm that the tampered transaction was classified as a client error rather than a server error, revealing the error classification boundary?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err.message contains JSON-breaking characters (e.g., unescaped quotes, backslashes), causing res.status(statusCode.code).json(errorMessageFormat(errorMessage)) to produce malformed JSON that breaks client-side transaction error parsing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err is null or undefined, causing handleError() to throw a TypeError when accessing err.message or err instanceof checks, resulting in an unhandled error within the error handler that leaves the HTTP response incomplete?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err.message contains the string 'statement timeout' (matching simpleErrors regex), causing shouldPrintStacktrace() to return false and the logger to only log err.message instead of the full err object, reducing the diagnostic information available to detect the tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err.constructor.name is a very long string (e.g., 10MB), causing the logger.warn template literal to attempt to write a massive log entry, exhausting log buffer memory and causing a denial-of-service?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction error where errorMessages is an array with thousands of elements, causing errorMessageFormat() to call .map() over all elements and produce a response with thousands of error messages, creating a response amplification that exhausts client-side memory when parsing the transaction error?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where req.originalUrl contains a very long string (e.g., 1MB URL), causing the logger template literal to write a massive log entry, exhausting log storage and hiding subsequent transaction tampering attempts in log noise?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err.message contains ANSI escape codes, causing the logger.warn/error output to manipulate terminal display of log entries, hiding transaction tampering evidence from operators monitoring logs in real-time?
